questionText: In the circuit shown in Figure 19.64, current flows through the 5.00 Ω resistor in the direction shown, and this resistor is measured to be consuming energy at a rate of 20.0 W. The batteries have negligibly small internal resistance. What current does the ammeter A read?

In the circuit shown in Figure 19.65, the 6.0 Ω resistor is consuming energy at a rate of 24 J/s when the current through it flows as shown. (a) Find the current through the ammeter A. (b) What are the polarity and emf $\mathcal{E}$ of the battery, assuming it has negligible internal resistance?

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the current through the ammeter A.

parts.label: b

questionText: A $500.0\Omega$ resistor is connected in series with a capacitor. What must be the capacitance of the capacitor to produce a time constant of $2.00\mathrm{s}$?

A fully charged $6.0\mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is connected in series with a $1.5\times 10^{5}\Omega$ resistor. What percentage of the original charge is left on the capacitor after $1.8\mathrm{s}$ of discharging?

A $12.4\mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is connected through a $0.895\mathrm{M}\Omega$ resistor to a constant potential difference of $60.0\mathrm{V}$. (a) Compute the charge on the capacitor at the following times after the connections are made: 0, 5.0 s, 10.0 s, 20.0 s, and 100.0 s. (b) Compute the charging currents at the same instants. (c) Graph the results of parts (a) and (b) for $t$ between 0 and $20\mathrm{s}$.

parts.label: a
parts.question: Compute the charge on the capacitor at the following times after the connections are made: 0, 5.0 s, 10.0 s, 20.0 s, and 100.0 s.

parts.label: b
parts.question: Compute the charging currents at the same instants.

parts.label: c
parts.question: Graph the results of parts (a) and (b) for $t$ between 0 and $20\mathrm{s}$.

questionText: A $6.00\mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor that is initially uncharged is connected in series with a $4500\Omega$ resistor and a $500\mathrm{V}$ emf source with negligible internal resistance. Just after the circuit is completed, what are (a) the voltage drop across the capacitor, (b) the voltage drop across the resistor, (c) the charge on the capacitor, and (d) the current through the resistor? (e) A long time after the circuit is completed (after many time constants), what are the values of the preceding four quantities?

parts.label: a
parts.question: the voltage drop across the capacitor,

parts.label: b
parts.question: the voltage drop across the resistor,

parts.label: c
parts.question: the charge on the capacitor, and

parts.label: d
parts.question: the current through the resistor?

parts.question: A long time after the circuit is completed (after many time constants), what are the values of the preceding four quantities?

questionText: Charging and discharging a capacitor. A $1.50\mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is charged through a $125\Omega$ resistor and then discharged through the same resistor by short-circuiting the battery. While the capacitor is being charged, find (a) the time for the charge on its plates to reach $1 - 1 / e$ of its maximum value and (b) the current in the circuit at that time. (c) During the discharge of the capacitor, find the time for the charge on its plates to decrease to $1 / e$ of its initial value. Also, find the time for the current in the circuit to decrease to $1 / e$ of its initial value.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the time for the charge on its plates to reach $1 - 1 / e$ of its maximum value

parts.label: b
parts.question: the current in the circuit at that time

parts.label: c
parts.question: During the discharge of the capacitor, find the time for the charge on its plates to decrease to $1 / e$ of its initial value. Also, find the time for the current in the circuit to decrease to $1 / e$ of its initial value.

questionText: Graphing the charge on a capacitor as a function of time. An initially uncharged capacitor $C$ charges through a resistor $R$ for many time constants and then discharges through the same resistor. Call $Q_{\mathrm{max}}$ the maximum charge on its plates and $I_{\mathrm{max}}$ the maximum current in the circuit. (a) Sketch clear graphs of the charge on the plates and the current in the circuit as functions of time for the charging process. (b) During the discharging process, the charge on the capacitor and the current both decrease exponentially from their maximum values. Use this fact to sketch graphs of the current in the circuit and the charge on the capacitor as functions of time.

parts.label: a
parts.question: Sketch clear graphs of the charge on the plates and the current in the circuit as functions of time for the charging process.

parts.label: b
parts.question: During the discharging process, the charge on the capacitor and the current both decrease exponentially from their maximum values. Use this fact to sketch graphs of the current in the circuit and the charge on the capacitor as functions of time.

questionText: If an ohmmeter is connected between points $a$ and $b$ in each of the circuits shown in Figure 19.68, what will it read?

A refrigerator draws 3.5 A of current while operating on a 120 V power line. If the refrigerator runs 50% of the time and electric power costs $0.12 per kWh, how much does it cost to run this refrigerator for a 30-day month?

A toaster using a Nichrome heating element operates on 120 V. When it is switched on at 20°C, the heating element carries an initial current of 1.35 A. A few seconds later, the current reaches the steady value of 1.23 A. (a) What is the final temperature of the element? The average value of the temperature coefficient of resistivity for Nichrome over the temperature range from 20°C to the final temperature of the element is $4.5 \times 10^{-4} (\mathrm{C}^{\circ})^{-1}$. (b) What is the power dissipated in the heating element (i) initially; (ii) when the current reaches a steady value?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the final temperature of the element? The average value of the temperature coefficient of resistivity for Nichrome over the temperature range from 20°C to the final temperature of the element is $4.5 \times 10^{-4} (\mathrm{C}^{\circ})^{-1}$.

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the power dissipated in the heating element (i) initially; (ii) when the current reaches a steady value?

questionText: A piece of wire has a resistance $R$. It is cut into three pieces of equal length, and the pieces are twisted together parallel to each other. What is the resistance of the resulting wire in terms of $R$?

Flashlight batteries. A typical small flashlight contains two batteries, each having an emf of 1.5 V, connected in series with a bulb having resistance 17 Ω. (a) If the internal resistance of the batteries is negligible, what power is delivered to the bulb? (b) If the batteries last for 5.0 h, what is the total energy delivered to the bulb? (c) The resistance of real batteries increases as they run down. If the initial internal resistance is negligible, what is the combined internal resistance of both batteries when the power to the bulb has decreased to half its initial value? (Assume that the resistance of the bulb is constant. Actually, it will change somewhat when the current through the filament changes because this changes the temperature of the filament and hence the resistivity of the filament wire.)

parts.label: a
parts.question: If the internal resistance of the batteries is negligible, what power is delivered to the bulb?

parts.label: b
parts.question: If the batteries last for 5.0 h, what is the total energy delivered to the bulb?

parts.label: c
parts.question: The resistance of real batteries increases as they run down. If the initial internal resistance is negligible, what is the combined internal resistance of both batteries when the power to the bulb has decreased to half its initial value?

questionText: In the circuit of Figure 19.69, find (a) the current through the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor, (b) the total rate of dissipation of electrical energy in the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor and in the internal resistance of the batteries, and (c) the total power being delivered by the two batteries.

parts.label: a

questionText: BIO Struck by lightning. Lightning strikes can involve currents as high as 25,000 A that last for about $40\,\mu\mathrm{s}$. If a person is struck by a bolt of lightning with these properties, the current will pass through his body. We shall assume that his mass is $75\,\mathrm{kg}$, that he is wet (after all, he is in a rainstorm) and therefore has a resistance of $1.0\,\mathrm{k}\Omega$, and that his body is all water (which is reasonable for a rough, but plausible, approximation). (a) By how many degrees Celsius would this lightning bolt increase the temperature of 75 kg of water? (b) Given that the internal body temperature is about $37^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, would the person's temperature actually increase that much? Why or why not? What would happen first?
expectedAnswer: a) 80 C°  b) Part of the person's body will be vaporized.

parts.label: a
parts.question: By how many degrees Celsius would this lightning bolt increase the temperature of 75 kg of water?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Given that the internal body temperature is about $37^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, would the person's temperature actually increase that much? Why or why not? What would happen first?

questionText: Each of the three resistors in Figure 19.70 has a resistance of $2.00\,\Omega$ and can dissipate a maximum of $32.0\,\mathrm{W}$ without becoming excessively heated. What is the maximum power the circuit can dissipate?

Energy use of home appliances. An 1800 W toaster, a 1400 W electric frying pan, and a 75 W lamp are plugged into the same electrical outlet in a 20 A, 120 V circuit. (Note: When plugged into the same outlet, the three devices are in parallel with each other across the 120 V outlet.) (a) What current is drawn by each device? (b) Will this combination blow the circuit breaker?
expectedAnswer: a) Toaster: 15.0 A, Frypan: 11.7 A, Lamp: 0.625 A  b) 27.3 A, circuit breaker will blow

parts.label: a
parts.question: What current is drawn by each device?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Will this combination blow the circuit breaker?

questionText: Two identical 1.00 Ω wires are laid side by side and soldered together so that they touch each other for half of their lengths. (See Figure 19.71.) What is the equivalent resistance of this combination?
